content.subtitle: Main pattern
items: Use a reporting verb + (that) + clause when we report a statement from a different time or viewpoint.
When the reporting verb is in the past, tense backshift is often needed.
examples: Direct: "I feel confident about the exam." → Reported: She said (that) she **felt** confident about the exam.
Direct: "We have completed the draft." → Reported: They explained (that) they **had completed** the draft.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Backshift is often optional when the fact is still true in the present.
In informal spoken English, speakers may keep the original tense if timing is clear.
Do not force backshift after reporting verbs in the present (say, tells, explains).

content.subtitle: Question structure
items: Yes/No questions: ask + if/whether + clause.
Wh-questions: ask + wh-word + clause.
Word order changes to statement order (no auxiliary inversion).
examples: "Did you submit the form?" → He asked **if I had submitted** the form.
"Where are they meeting?" → She asked **where they were meeting**.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Do not keep the question mark or direct-question word order inside the reported clause.
Use whether (rather than if) after prepositions and before to-infinitive patterns.
Short answers in direct speech usually become full clauses in reported speech.

content.subtitle: Infinitive patterns
rows: "Close the door." → The teacher told us to close the door.
"Could you wait a moment?" → She asked me to wait a moment.
"Don't interrupt." → He told them not to interrupt.
"Take backup notes." → She advised us to take backup notes.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Do not use imperative form directly inside reported speech clauses.
Choose reporting verbs carefully: ask (request), tell/order (strong instruction), advise (suggestion).

sections.title: Pronouns, determiners, time and place references

content.subtitle: Reference changes
content.description: When viewpoint changes, pronouns and context words often need adjustment.
rows: the previous day / the day before

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Not every item has to change; keep original references when location and timing are still the same.
Prioritise clarity: choose forms that avoid ambiguity in long reported narratives.

content.subtitle: Pattern families
items: verb + that-clause: admit, claim, deny, insist, mention
verb + object + infinitive: advise, remind, warn, persuade
verb + -ing form: deny, suggest, recommend
examples: They **insisted that** the data was reliable.
The editor **reminded us to** verify every source.

content.subtitle: Register and tone
items: Formal reports often use neutral verbs (state, confirm, indicate).
Narrative writing may prefer expressive verbs (mutter, whisper, protest).
Select verbs that reflect attitude without changing the original meaning.

content.subtitle: Frequent errors
items: Mixing direct and reported structures in the same sentence.
Keeping original tense, pronouns and time words when the context has shifted.
Using an unsuitable reporting verb for the speaker's intention.
Forgetting statement word order in reported questions.

content.subtitle: Checklist
items: Identify who is speaking and from which time perspective.
Check tense, modal, pronoun and time/place consistency.
Confirm that meaning, tone and level of certainty remain accurate.
